commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=3ab1cead007c6522eb4e540038c5a8832b038e69)
commands.review_summary:
The PR version-scopes Codex app inventory cache keys and changes Codex plugin app config, persisted policy context, resume replay, and tests to preserve thread reviewers while routing only always-destructive app approvals to user review.

PR surface: Source +226, Tests +399. Total +625 across 11 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review finding from source inspection: the PR calls mcpServerStatus/list without threadId before a thread exists, and Codex source/tests show threadless status can omit thread-scoped tools. The original reviewer-policy bug also has focused tests and partial live proof, but final-head live proof is still incomplete.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Protected maintainer-labeled PR needs author or maintainer revision for the inventory-scope blocker and final-head real behavior proof before merge.

target.title: fix(codex): preserve per-app approval reviewer policy
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where enabling Codex plugins could overwrite a thread's approval reviewer globally, causing non-destructive app reads to inherit user review and making resumed or recovered threads diverge from the original per-app policy. It also fixes stale Codex app inventories after plugin upgrades.

## Why This Change Was Made

Plugin configuration now leaves the top-level reviewer untouched and assigns `approvals_reviewer: "user"` only to concrete enabled apps whose resolved destructive-action mode is `always`. The same resolved policy is persisted and replayed for resume and recovery, while the inventory cache key includes the app/plugin version so upgraded inventories refresh.

The destructive-tool prompt inventory follows the Codex app-server approval predicate, accepts tool ownership only from the `codex_apps` MCP server, and requests only tool/auth status. Existing app enablement, destructive/open-world flags, tool approval modes, activation validation, and fail-closed defaults remain intact.

AI-assisted: yes. Codex implemented the changes and completed a clean repository autoreview.

## User Impact

Calendar and other read-only app actions can continue without an unnecessary user-review override. Apps with an effective `allow_destructive_actions = "always"` still route destructive approvals to the user at the app boundary, while `auto`, `allow`, and `deny` inherit the thread's configured reviewer. The behavior remains consistent after thread resume, recovery, and replay.

## Evidence

- Focused plugin/thread lifecycle tests: 3 files passed, 141 tests passed.
- Focused run-attempt resume-policy test: 1 passed, 114 skipped by the test filter.
- File-scoped extension lint: passed.
- Repository autoreview: clean, with no accepted or actionable findings.
- Live Docker/Slack probe on the initial reviewer patch: Google Calendar read returned `CALENDAR_READ_OK 7` without elicitation. A Calendar write reached `create_event`, was rejected by the configured auto reviewer, emitted no Slack elicitation, and created no event.

Commands:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.binding.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts

OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-run-attempt-reviewer-fixed node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -t "passes configured app-server policy, sandbox, service tier, and model on resume"

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-startup.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.binding.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts

.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --prompt-file /tmp/openclaw-codex-reviewer-autoreview.md --stream-engine-output
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Codex plugin setup must not force the root reviewer or `_default` reviewer; only concrete enabled apps with effective destructive mode `always` receive the user reviewer. App inventory must refresh after plugin version changes and persisted policy must replay identically.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw Docker image `openclaw-debug:per-app-reviewer-v2-20260629t0553` (`linux/amd64`) with a healthy gateway at `127.0.0.1:18789`, exercised through the `kevinlin` Slack/OpenClaw integration profile. The remote `openclaw-gateway-kevinlin` StatefulSet was scaled to zero during the test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built and ran the local image, sent a Google Calendar read request and a Google Calendar event-create request through the Slack integration, then ran the focused Vitest, oxlint, and autoreview commands listed above against the final commit.
- **Evidence after fix:** The read replied `CALENDAR_READ_OK 7` with no elicitation. The write invoked `create_event`; the app-server reported `user rejected MCP tool call`, no Slack elicitation appeared, and no event was created. Final focused tests passed 141/141, the filtered run-attempt test passed, oxlint passed, and autoreview was clean.
- **Observed result after fix:** Read-only Calendar use no longer elicited in the live path. Destructive-write policy reached the app approval boundary without silently creating an event. Unit coverage confirms reviewer omission for root, `_default`, disabled patches, and all non-`always` effective modes, plus per-app user review for inherited or explicit `always` across initial and reconstructed patches.
- **What was not tested:** The live Docker/Slack probe used the initial reviewer-policy patch before the final tool-name, MCP-server ownership, approval-predicate, status-detail, and turn-override hardening commits. Those final changes were validated by focused tests, lint, and autoreview but were not rebuilt and rerun in Docker. A successful Calendar write was not observed because the configured auto reviewer rejected the tool call. The full `run-attempt.test.ts` file was not green locally: three environment-dependent tool-policy tests saw the local computer-use/MCP delegation configuration; the directly affected resume-policy case passed. Local `pnpm check:changed` was blocked before checks by a registry `403` fetching `acpx`.
target.author: kevinslin

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
